source_signals.title: Weather tracker: hail covers parts of Tunisia and Algeria like snow
source_signals.summary: Accumulations of up to 3cm deep reported as severe thunderstorms also bring heavy downpours to central Italy Severe thunderstorms have affected the Mediterranean this week. On Monday, a surface low-pressure system in the Mediterranean in conjunction with an upper air cut-off low, led to thunderstorms over north Africa. Their intensity was aided by the hot precursor conditions. Algeria and Tunisia were notably affected by the thunderstorms, with some hail accumulation layers as a result. When so much hail forms, it starts to lay down sheets of hail, covering the ground like snow. Hail accumulations of up to 3cm were reported in Oum Ladjoul and Hammam Sokhna in Algeria, and there were hailstones of up to 3cm in diameter in Makthar, Tunisia. Thunderstorms continued in the region through the following day, with further hail accumulations, notably in Ouled Bousmir, Tunisia, where there was a layer about 2cm deep. Continue reading...

Irene has stopped by a hidden gambling table in a western suburb of Honiara to play Pass, a street card game gaining popularity in the Solomon Islands capital. Continue reading...

The male wolf, named Neukgu, burrowed out of his enclosure at the O-World zoo in Daejeon on 8 April. Animal rights activists questioned whether the wolf could survive outside the zoo and also worried he might be killed during capture, something that happened to a puma that escaped from the same zoo in 2018. source_signals.title: Ben Roberts-Smith’s comrades say he ordered them to execute unarmed civilians, court documents show
source_signals.summary: Former SAS corporal allegedly placed man on his knees and ordered fellow soldier to shoot him, according to statement of facts Australian soldiers have told prosecutors they executed unarmed civilians at the orders of Ben Roberts-Smith or in complicity with him, according to a statement of facts tendered to the New South Wales local court. Roberts-Smith, a Victoria Cross recipient and once one of Australia’s most lionised soldiers, faces five charges of the war crime of murder, allegedly committed while he served in the Australian SAS in Afghanistan. Each victim was unarmed and present in a location where Roberts-Smith could reasonably have suspected insurgents to be located; Each offence was committed in a situation where there was no active engagements with enemy forces and the Australian Defence Force was in control of the environment; Evidence was planted or falsely associated with each deceased to enhance reporting that each of the killings was within the lawful rules of engagement; Each deceased was handcuffed, detained for a period, and questioned prior to their execution; None of the deceased was killed in a situation where the Australian Defence Force did not have effective control of the battlespace. Continue reading...

source_signals.title: Traders placed over $1bn in perfectly timed bets on the Iran war. What is going on?
source_signals.summary: Suspicious wagers on the US-Israel war in Iran are creating huge windfalls and raising concerns among lawmakers Sixteen bets made $100,000 accurately predicting the timing of the US airstrikes against Iran on 27 February. Later, a single user would make over $550,000 after betting that Ayatollah Ali Khamenei would topple, just moments before his assassination by Israeli forces. On 7 April, right before Donald Trump announced a temporary ceasefire with Iran, traders bet $950m that oil prices would come down. They did. These bets and other well-timed wagers accurately predicted the precise timing of major developments in the US-Israel war with Iran, creating huge windfalls and raising concerns among lawmakers and experts over potential insider trading. Continue reading...

source_signals.title: Trump’s new budget ignores dying Americans and gives away record sums to the US military
source_signals.summary: President’s proposed budget slashes health department by 12% while throwing $1.5tn – a 42% increase – to the military Americans are dying in droves. Deaths due to avoidable causes in the United States –which could be dealt with via prevention or proper healthcare – far outpace those in most of country’s peers in the industrialized world. Most notably, Americans die of treatable conditions at nearly twice the rate as Spaniards, French, Japanese and Australians. They would most likely live longer if they enjoyed better access to healthcare. Americans are the most likely to skip a doctor’s appointment due to its cost, the most likely to skip a medical test and to skimp on prescription drugs . This is unsurprising, given the extraordinary lack of public health insurance in the United States. Americans face the highest out-of-pocket expenses for medical services in their peer group. Continue reading...

It is another win for the lobbyists behind Dallas’s “Y’all Street” – the Texan city’s aggressive push to steal New York’s financial crown. Continue reading...

source_signals.title: Tech oligarchs reshape humanity while billionaires of old seem quaint
source_signals.summary: From Gates to Musk and Altman, today’s ultra-rich steer AI and tech, raising questions about who decides the future When Bill Gates became the first modern IT mogul to reach the apex of wealth and power in 1992, the world was a very different place. Gates joined the top 10 on Forbes magazine’s billionaires list alongside Japanese, German, Canadian, South Korean and Swedish billionaires, including those with family fortunes from Britain and America. A broad mix of industries was on the list: Retail and media, property management and packaging, an investment firm and a couple of industrial conglomerates. Their fortunes almost added up to $100bn – equivalent to about 0.4% of the US’s GDP that year. The oligarchy has changed drastically since then. Bernard Arnault, of French luxury group LVMH, Amancio Ortega, the Spanish clothing mogul, and Warren Buffett, the US investor, were the only old-school billionaires among the top 10 in 2025. The rest largely made their money from high-tech: Elon Musk, Jeff Bezos, Mark Zuckerberg, Larry Ellison, Steve Ballmer and Google’s Sergey Brin and Larry Page. The top 10 amassed over $16trn, which is about 8% of US GDP. Continue reading...

He told BBC Radio 4’s Today programme: I completely refute the suggestion the PM misled the public or the House of Commons. source_signals.title: Screenmaxxing: why Hollywood is supersizing the big screen experience
source_signals.summary: With Imax more popular than ever, a new way to watch movies – HDR by Barco – has been quietly rolling out but what difference does it really make? At this year’s CinemaCon, an annual gathering where film studios show off their upcoming wares to excite the exhibitors they hope to showcase them, Disney announced a new way to see a movie, sort of: InfinityVision . Despite the cutesy Marvelized name, it’s not a superhero-specific experience; it’s a certification for premium large-format (PLF) auditoriums. The idea is that any InfinityVision-certified screen will adhere to or exceed standards – vaguely described so far – in size, sound quality, and picture brightness/clarity. There are supposedly 300 such screens already certified around the globe, though there doesn’t seem to be an actual list explaining which ones they are yet. The practical reason for this additional layer of branding is that Disney’s Avengers: Doomsday is premiering in December on the same weekend as the third Dune movie, which has a deal to occupy coveted (and limited) Imax screens for several weeks. This essentially locks Earth’s mightiest heroes out of one of the marquee names in exhibition; InfinityVision seems intended to reassure viewers that their other options, presumably the various Dolby, RPX, and other branded PLF auditoriums that already exist, are as impressive as possible. Call it screenmaxxing. Continue reading...

source_signals.title: Now you can break up with big tech at a bar: ‘cybersecurity disguised as a party’

While she’ll probably do the latter, she’s also a cybersecurity organizer leading the evening’s event. Thompson is the host, along with the New York City-based tech organizing coalition Cypurr Collective, of Break Up With Google . Its purpose isn’t a mystery; the main goal is to help attenders understand how to mitigate their vulnerability to surveillance through major tech services. But it’s also important for people to have fun while they do it, Thompson said – hence the DJs playing until the wee hours of the morning. Continue reading...

United were newly crowned champions of Europe – finally – and the world appeared to be their oyster. But of course they wouldn’t pick up another trophy until winning the Second Division championship in 1975. This was the first sign that a mild complacency had set in at Old Trafford. “Chelsea surely will never gain two points so easily again,” reported the Guardian, “and at the same time it is difficult to visualise United being so abysmally poor.” The visitors employed a power game – Ron Harris, Eddie McCreadie and David Webb crunching in with hard tackles, winning almost every ball, and shipping it forward quickly. Tommy Baldwin put Chelsea in front within 40 seconds. Bobby Tambling snaffled a ridiculous clearance by Tony Dunne to make it 2-0 on 13 minutes. Baldwin made it three seven minutes before half-time. source_signals.summary: The three-time Olympic champion is brilliant, charismatic, relatable, basically the best British athlete of all-time. But he’s also a victim of the decline of minority sports The Austrian philosopher and novelist Robert Musil once wrote a lengthy meditation on human capacity based around seeing the phrase “a racehorse of genius” in a newspaper sports section. Musil was disturbed by this idea. His basic question was: can a horse really be a genius? If we are to ascribe the label of genius to a horse, based on its ability to run fast and successfully eat oats, where does this leave the unmapped capacities of the actual human genius? What is consciousness? What is a human? Should the question in fact be: will there ever be a human of sufficient genius they are able to actually perceive the genius of a horse? Continue reading...

England’s women delivered a dominant Six Nations win over Scotland, but the sport desk will stay tightly focused on the Premier League and major football state of play.

lead_story.title: At least six killed in Kyiv as gunman opens fire and takes hostages
lead_story.summary: At least six people were killed in Kyiv after a gunman opened fire inside a supermarket, took hostages and was later killed in a shoot-out with police. The attack adds to an already volatile security picture in Ukraine’s capital and will raise questions about public safety, policing and motive.
lead_story.source_signal_title: At least six killed in Kyiv as gunman opens fire and takes hostages
body: At least six people were killed in Kyiv after a gunman opened fire inside a supermarket, took hostages and was later killed in a shoot-out with police. The violence in Ukraine’s capital has shocked residents and left key questions unanswered about the attacker’s motive.

Officials said the gunman was inside the store when the shooting began and later took hostages. Police intervened and the attacker died during the exchange of fire, but the circumstances are still being pieced together.

The incident is a serious security shock for Kyiv, where daily life has already been shaped by the wider war. It may also renew debate over whether enough is being done to protect crowded civilian spaces.

For now, the main facts are limited: multiple deaths, a hostage situation and a police operation that ended with the suspect dead. Investigators will now try to establish whether this was an isolated act of violence or something more deliberate.

The case is likely to keep attention on policing, emergency response and public safety in the capital. It also serves as a reminder that, even in a city accustomed to danger, sudden violence can still strike without warning.

stories.title: French peacekeeper killed in southern Lebanon
stories.summary: A French peacekeeper has been killed in southern Lebanon, with President Emmanuel Macron blaming Hezbollah and the armed group denying any connection to the attack. The case underlines how fragile the security environment remains even after a ceasefire.
stories.source_signal_title: French peacekeeper killed in southern Lebanon
body: A French peacekeeper has been killed in southern Lebanon, deepening anxiety over the fragility of the security situation there even after a ceasefire. President Emmanuel Macron has blamed Hezbollah for the attack, while the armed group has denied any connection.

The killing is likely to sharpen international concern about the safety of peacekeepers operating along the frontier, where tensions have remained high despite efforts to contain the fighting. Such incidents can quickly threaten the credibility of ceasefire arrangements and the forces meant to uphold them.

Hezbollah’s denial leaves a contested account at the centre of the case, and it is not yet clear whether the attack was deliberate, a misidentification or part of a wider escalation. That uncertainty matters because blame in Lebanon often becomes part of the conflict itself.

French forces have long been involved in peacekeeping missions in the region, and any fatality carries political as well as military weight in Paris. Macron’s public accusation suggests France will want answers quickly.
The episode underlines how unstable southern Lebanon remains, with even a pause in open fighting failing to remove the risks to international personnel and civilians alike.

stories.title: Hungary’s next PM is a man in a hurry
stories.summary: After Péter Magyar’s dramatic victory, Hungary enters a period of rapid political reordering as he prepares for a transfer of power and tries to turn an electoral landslide into durable control.
stories.source_signal_title: Orbán's era was over in a flash and Hungary's next PM is a man in a hurry
body: Péter Magyar has moved quickly to turn his dramatic victory into a workable plan for government, as Hungary begins to reckon with the prospect of an orderly transfer of power after years under Viktor Orbán. The speed is striking: rather than waiting for the political dust to settle, Magyar’s Tisza party is already preparing for what comes next.

That urgency reflects both opportunity and risk. Landslide victories can create momentum, but they can also fade if a new force cannot translate public anger into durable institutions, disciplined staffing and a coherent agenda.

Hungary now enters a period of political reordering in which Magyar will need to prove that he can govern, not just oppose. The task is made harder by the scale of the system he would inherit, including a state apparatus shaped over many years by Orbán’s allies.
The bigger question is whether Magyar can convert a decisive electoral moment into lasting control before his opponents regroup. In that sense, the pace itself is part of the strategy.
For voters, the promise is a break with the old order; for Hungary’s political class, the challenge is to see whether the next chapter can be written as quickly as the first one changed.

stories.title: Pope says 'tyrants' speech was not aimed at Trump
stories.summary: Pope Francis has said his remarks about “tyrants” were misinterpreted after a public spat with Donald Trump, attempting to lower the temperature after the exchange drew wide attention.
stories.source_signal_title: Pope says 'tyrants' speech was not aimed at Trump
body: Pope Francis has said his remarks about “tyrants” were misinterpreted after a public clash with Donald Trump, in an effort to cool a row that drew wide attention. The pontiff’s clarification suggests he wants to lower the temperature after a brief but visible transatlantic spat.

The dispute mattered because it touched on the relationship between religion and politics, and on the influence the pope still wields when he speaks about leaders and power. Even a short exchange can quickly become part of a wider culture war.

Francis’s explanation appears intended to narrow the dispute rather than prolong it. He has not changed the substance of his concerns, but he is signalling that his comments were not a direct attack on the US president.

The episode also shows how quickly papal remarks can be folded into partisan argument, especially in the US. That leaves room for both sides to claim they have been misunderstood, while the original message risks getting lost.
For now, the Vatican seems to be trying to draw a line under the argument before it hardens into a larger diplomatic or political issue.

body: At least six people were killed in Kyiv after a gunman opened fire inside a supermarket, took hostages and was later killed in a shoot-out with police. The attack has left the Ukrainian capital facing another sudden security scare.

Police said the suspect died during the operation, but the motive has not been established. That means there is still no clear answer to whether the violence was personal, criminal or something else entirely.
The incident matters because it comes in a city already under extraordinary pressure from war. Even if the attack is unrelated to the conflict, its effect on public confidence will be immediate.

Authorities are likely to come under scrutiny over how quickly they moved, what warning signs there were and whether more could have been done to protect shoppers. The official account may yet develop as investigators review the scene.
For now, Kyiv is left with an unsettling combination of shock, grief and uncertainty.

stories.source_signal_title: At least six killed in Kyiv as gunman opens fire and takes hostages
body: At least six people were killed in Kyiv after a gunman opened fire inside a supermarket, took hostages and was later killed in a shoot-out with police. The capital of Ukraine has been shaken by a rare mass hostage-taking in a public place.

Officials said police killed the gunman after the standoff. The cause of the attack is still unknown, and that uncertainty is likely to keep attention on the investigation rather than on any broader theory about the event.

The case is significant because it highlights how quickly a single violent episode can unsettle a city already dealing with war-related danger. Public safety, not just battlefield security, is now part of the strain on Kyiv.
The next step will be to establish the attacker’s identity, his movements and whether he had any known grievances or links. Until then, speculation is likely to be more abundant than facts.
This is a grim reminder that civilian life in Kyiv remains vulnerable to sudden violence, even outside the front line.

stories.title: Oil price drops below $90 a barrel after Iran says strait of Hormuz is open
stories.summary: Brent crude fell below $90 a barrel after Iran said the Strait of Hormuz was open for commercial shipping during the ceasefire. The move eases immediate pressure on energy markets, but prices remain sensitive to any renewed disruption.
stories.source_signal_title: Oil price drops below $90 a barrel after Iran says strait of Hormuz is open
body: Oil prices fell after Iran said the Strait of Hormuz was open for commercial shipping during the ceasefire, easing some of the immediate pressure on energy markets. Brent crude dropped below $90 a barrel, a sign that traders were ready to respond quickly to even a temporary reduction in risk.

The strait is one of the world’s most important oil chokepoints, so any threat to shipping can rattle markets far beyond the Gulf. When Iran signalled that vessels could transit the waterway, traders pushed prices lower and gas markets followed.

The relief may be short-lived. The ceasefire is only temporary, and any renewed disruption could send prices back up just as fast. That means consumers, airlines and governments are still exposed to volatility.

The drop also reflects how closely energy markets are now tied to the wider conflict. As long as the war remains unresolved, shipping, fuel costs and inflation expectations will remain sensitive to political headlines.
For now, the opening of the strait has taken some heat out of the market, but it has not removed the underlying risk.

stories.title: Central bank bosses enlist for war game to gauge threat of Lehman-style bust
stories.summary: Senior officials from the UK, US and EU will take part in a Washington exercise on Saturday to test how they would respond to the collapse of a globally significant bank. The scenario shows how concerned regulators remain about financial stability.
stories.source_signal_title: Central bank bosses enlist for war game to gauge threat of Lehman-style bust
body: Top central bankers and finance ministers from the UK, US and EU are due to take part in a Washington war game on Saturday designed to test their response to the collapse of a globally significant bank. The exercise is a reminder of how seriously regulators still treat the risk of another Lehman-style shock.

The scenario is meant to expose how authorities would coordinate under pressure if a major institution began to fail. Officials involved include the Federal Reserve, the European Central Bank and the Bank of England, where governor Andrew Bailey is expected to take part.

That matters because the memory of the 2008 financial crisis still shapes regulation, liquidity planning and cross-border crisis management. Even after years of tighter oversight, policymakers remain wary of hidden vulnerabilities in the global banking system.

The exercise is not a prediction of imminent trouble, but it does show that unease has returned to the top of the system. Regulators are trying to understand whether they could move quickly enough if a crisis hit.
What happens in the drill will not calm markets on its own, but it may reveal how prepared the major economies really are for a fast-moving banking failure.

stories.title: Mortgage rates show signs of falling after Iran war peak
stories.summary: Major lenders are trimming mortgage rates as markets take some comfort from the ceasefire signal in the Iran war. The question now is whether the relief proves durable enough to ease borrowing costs more broadly.
stories.source_signal_title: Mortgage rates show signs of falling after Iran war peak
body: Major lenders are beginning to trim mortgage rates as markets take some comfort from the signal of a possible truce in the Iran war. The change offers a small but welcome sign that borrowing costs could ease if the geopolitical shock continues to unwind.

The fall is linked to expectations that pressure on oil and wider financial markets may be less severe than feared. When energy prices and bond yields stabilise, lenders often have room to adjust mortgage offers downward.

For households, the question is whether this is a temporary reprieve or the start of something more durable. Mortgage rates have been volatile for years, and another flare-up in the conflict could quickly reverse the improvement.

The broader issue is that housing costs remain highly sensitive to developments far beyond the property market. A ceasefire or peace signal can ease pressure, but the transmission to family finances is indirect and uncertain.
Lenders are moving cautiously, which suggests they see the same risk as borrowers: relief may be real, but it is not yet secure.

stories.title: UK economy grew faster than expected in February ahead of Iran war
stories.summary: The UK economy expanded faster than expected in February, recording its biggest monthly rise in more than two years before the outbreak of the US-Israeli war with Iran. The timing matters because the shock since then may yet weigh on the next set of figures.
stories.source_signal_title: UK economy grew faster than expected in February ahead of Iran war
body: The UK economy grew faster than expected in February, recording its biggest monthly rise in more than two years before the outbreak of the US-Israeli war with Iran. The timing matters because the shock that followed could yet weigh on the next set of figures.

The stronger-than-expected monthly performance suggests the economy was on firmer footing than many analysts had assumed. But that is only part of the picture, because official data now have to absorb the impact of the conflict and its effect on energy, confidence and trade.

For households and businesses, the key issue is whether the February strength can survive the disruption that followed. If the war has pushed up fuel costs or dampened investment, later numbers may look much weaker.
The latest data therefore carry a warning as well as some good news. They show momentum before the crisis, but not yet the full cost of what came after.
Economists will be watching the next releases closely to see whether the conflict has merely delayed growth or begun to erode it.

stories.title: UK prepares for food shortages in worst case scenario as Iran war continues
stories.summary: British ministers are drawing up contingency plans for possible food shortages if the Iran war keeps disrupting supply chains, including carbon dioxide used for chicken, pork and fizzy drinks. The government is trying to avoid panic while preparing for a worst-case scenario.

stories.title: Things can't go on like this with online safety, Starmer tells tech bosses
